items.q: Which of the 6 Rs has the GREATEST environmental benefit?
items.correct: Refuse or Reduce (preventing the impact at source).
items.why: Students treat recycling as the gold standard, but recycling uses energy and downgrades materials. The hierarchy is Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Recycle, Rethink - PREVENTION beats reprocessing.

items.q: What is the difference between a wood stain and a varnish?
items.correct: Stain colours the wood; varnish provides a protective surface coat.
items.random_wrong: Stain is permanent, varnish always peels off.
items.why: Students confuse colouring (stain penetrates) with protecting (varnish sits on top). Many products combine both, but their primary functions differ. Often stain is applied first, then varnish.

items.q: Why do designers often use 5th to 95th percentile data instead of just the 50th?
items.correct: To accommodate the majority of users rather than just the average.
items.misconception: Because the 50th percentile is too unreliable.
items.why: Students think designing for the average user is best, but it excludes most people. The 5th-95th range covers 90% of users - designing only for the mean fails everyone above or below it.
Correct answer: Classmates. “Yourself” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: The specification. “Your favourite colour” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many design-evaluation concepts are listed in this lesson?
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
